metadata.project: Unequal Visibility on Social Media & Political Dysfunction
thesis_under_review: 1. Platforms skew attention toward few users (descriptive).
2. Platform-attributable, not just user behavior (algorithm + network effects).
3. Unequal visibility leads to unequal agenda-setting power.
4. High-visibility users convert visibility into political power.
common_counters: platforms care about content not creators

fields.title: source title
fields.type: book | paper | chapter | report | essay | preprint
fields.priority: 1 = top must-read, 2 = cluster-level important, 3 = supporting, 4 = foundational/contextual
fields.areas: array from: smp-causes-unequal-vis | why-unequal-vis-happens | is-unequal-vis-bad | historical-baseline | counter-democratization
fields.stance: pro | anti | mixed | neutral (relative to the thesis above)
fields.method: rct | audit-sock-puppet | audit-crowdsourced | audit-scrape | survey | linked-panel | observational | computational | ethnography | interviews | theoretical | essay | review | historical | book | model | quasi-experiment | case-study | content-analysis | mixed-methods
fields.clusters: which cluster file(s) this appeared in
fields.verified: true if reachable DOI/URL confirmed; false if flagged for human re-check
fields.note: one-sentence summary of contribution to thesis

sources.title: The New Media Monopoly
sources.note: Quantitative anchor for legacy concentration: independent voices dropped from 50 firms in 1983 to 5 by 2004, establishing the pre-platform concentration baseline.

sources.title: Breaking the Social Media Prism: How to Make Our Platforms Less Polarizing
sources.note: Strongest articulation of the unequal-visibility mediator: extremists get amplified, moderates self-silence, producing misperceived opinion distributions.

sources.title: Supersharers of fake news on Twitter
sources.note: 2,107 supersharers = 80% of fake-news shares reaching 5% of voters — strongest direct support for unequal-visibility mediator at scale.

sources.title: Want to Be on the Top? Algorithmic Power and the Threat of Invisibility on Facebook
sources.note: Foundational reframing of platform discipline as the threat of perpetual invisibility under algorithmic ranking, anchoring the mechanism leg of the thesis.

sources.title: How Behavior Spreads: The Science of Complex Contagions
sources.note: Book-length synthesis showing consequential behaviors spread through clustered ties not broadcast hubs, splitting agenda-setting (hubs) from behavior-change (community).

sources.title: Playing the Visibility Game: How Digital Influencers and Algorithms Negotiate Influence on Instagram
sources.note: Anchor source treating algorithmic visibility as an asymmetric negotiated game between influencers and platforms, articulating mechanism with empirical grounding.

sources.title: The Structural Virality of Online Diffusion
sources.note: Billion-event Twitter analysis showing popularity is overwhelmingly broadcast-driven by a few large seeds rather than bottom-up viral cascades.

sources.title: Fake News on Twitter during the 2016 U.S. Presidential Election
sources.note: Voter-linked Twitter panel finding 1% of users absorbed 80% of fake news exposure and 0.1% drove 80% of sharing, evidencing extreme agenda-setting by a tiny minority.

sources.title: How do social media feed algorithms affect attitudes and behavior in an election campaign?
sources.note: Meta-academic 2020 RCT showing chronological feeds change exposure substantially but produce null effects on polarization and key attitudes over three months.

sources.title: Reshares on social media amplify political news but do not detectably affect beliefs or opinions
sources.note: Reshare-removal RCT cuts political news and untrustworthy content exposure dramatically yet finds no detectable attitude effects.

sources.title: The Myth of Digital Democracy
sources.note: First book-length empirical refutation of the 'internet democratizes voice' claim, showing online political audiences concentrate on top outlets and elite bloggers.

sources.title: The Internet Trap: How the Digital Economy Builds Monopolies and Undermines Democracy
sources.note: Argues digital attention is more concentrated than offline media because platform 'stickiness' compounds small early advantages into enormous winner-take-all gaps.

sources.title: Mass communication and para-social interaction: Observations on intimacy at a distance
sources.note: Foundational essay introducing parasocial interaction, providing the scalable trust mechanism that explains how visibility converts to influence.

sources.title: Algorithmic amplification of politics on Twitter
sources.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
sources.note: Twitter's internal ~2M-account RCT showing algorithmic timeline asymmetrically amplifies mainstream-right political content across six of seven countries.

sources.title: Social influencers and election outcomes
sources.note: Causal difference-in-differences study showing Rezo's viral video caused a 4.7pp shift in CDU/CSU vote share, the strongest contemporary causal estimate of influencer political power.

sources.title: A systematic review of worldwide causal and correlational evidence on digital media and democracy
sources.note: Reviews 496 studies: digital media benefits emerging democracies but harms established ones — the strongest defense against the Arab Spring counter.

sources.title: Political Turbulence: How Social Media Shape Collective Action
sources.note: Coins chaotic pluralism: skewed, few-winners-many-losers online participation as a legitimate model of democracy, the most direct doctrinal opponent to the project thesis.

sources.title: Rich Media, Poor Democracy: Communication Politics in Dubious Times
sources.note: Argues U.S. corporate media concentration was structurally incompatible with democracy long before platforms, establishing concentration as the legacy baseline.

sources.title: Like-minded sources on Facebook are prevalent but not polarizing
sources.note: RCT reducing like-minded source exposure on Facebook by a third produced no measurable change across eight attitudinal outcomes, complicating echo-chamber claims.

sources.title: Experimental Study of Inequality and Unpredictability in an Artificial Cultural Market
sources.note: Music Lab RCT showing social-influence visibility cues causally produce higher inequality and unpredictability of cultural success beyond quality.

sources.title: Twitter and Tear Gas: The Power and Fragility of Networked Protest
sources.note: Anchors the empirical engagement with the Arab-Spring counter by showing networked protest's fast visibility is structurally fragile under algorithmic gatekeepers.

sources.title: The spread of true and false news online
sources.note: False news diffused farther, faster, deeper than truth on Twitter; political falsehoods most viral, with humans (not bots) driving the asymmetry.

sources.title: How Trump drove coverage to the nomination: Hybrid media campaigning
sources.note: Canonical case study of one high-visibility user (Trump) converting Twitter attention into mainstream agenda dominance and formal political power.

sources.title: The welfare effects of social media
sources.note: 4-week Facebook deactivation RCT reduced polarization, increased well-being — counter to engagement = democracy framing.

sources.title: The effects of Facebook and Instagram on the 2020 election: A deactivation experiment
sources.note: Six-week paid deactivation RCT finds large reductions in political-news exposure but null effects on polarization and vote choice, a key counter to strong attitude-effects claims.

sources.title: The Long Tail: Why the Future of Business Is Selling Less of More
sources.note: Canonical counter-thesis claiming digital distribution fragments attention by lifting niche products into collectively dominant share.

sources.title: Exposure to opposing views on social media can increase political polarization
sources.note: Field experiment: paying partisans to follow opposing-view bots backfired — Republicans became more conservative, undermining naive cross-exposure remedies.

sources.title: Stewardship of global collective behavior
sources.note: Multidisciplinary manifesto arguing algorithmic platforms compress timescales of cumulative advantage beyond the window for institutional correction, framing the pace argument.

sources.title: Exposure to Ideologically Diverse News and Opinion on Facebook
sources.note: 10.1M-user Facebook study quantifying algorithmic ranking's reduction of cross-cutting exposure while authors emphasize user choice as the larger factor.

sources.title: Problematic machine behavior: A systematic literature review of algorithm audits
sources.venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction (CSCW)
sources.note: PRISMA review of 62 algorithm audits documenting recurring discrimination, distortion, exploitation, and misjudgment across platforms.

sources.title: Emergence of scaling in random networks
sources.note: Canonical preferential-attachment model showing power-law degree distributions emerge from generic growth + preferential linking, establishing the network-endogenous baseline for visibility skew.

sources.title: The Wealth of Networks: How Social Production Transforms Markets and Freedom
sources.note: Foundational case that networked peer production pluralizes discourse compared to mass-media gatekeepers; the strongest access-not-visibility argument.

sources.title: Network Propaganda: Manipulation, Disinformation, and Radicalization in American Politics
sources.note: Asymmetric polarization in U.S. media — right-wing sphere structurally insulated; social media amplifies but doesn't create the asymmetry.

sources.title: The Logic of Connective Action: Digital Media and the Personalization of Contentious Politics
sources.note: Distinguishes collective from connective action; inclusive personalized frames travel through social media without organizational sponsorship.

sources.title: Politics on Twitter: One-Third of Tweets from U.S. Adults Are Political
sources.note: Year-long analysis showing a quarter of US adult Twitter users produce 99% of political tweets, evidencing extreme agenda-setting concentration.

sources.title: A 61-million-person experiment in social influence and political mobilization
sources.note: A single Facebook nudge moved ~340,000 votes in 2010 — proof-of-concept that platform UI choices have non-trivial electoral effects without editorial checks.

sources.title: #Ferguson: Digital Protest, Hashtag Ethnography, and the Racial Politics of Social Media in the United States
sources.note: Theorizes hashtag ethnography after Ferguson, showing social media as strategic outlet for contesting mainstream coverage of racialized violence.

sources.title: The Image: A Guide to Pseudo-Events in America
sources.note: Documents celebrity-as-political-power and 'well-knownness' feedback loops operating fully on legacy media 60+ years before social platforms.

sources.title: Symbolic Power
sources.note: Bridges visibility-as-being-seen with visibility-as-naming-power, supplying the conceptual link between visibility and agenda-setting authority central to the thesis.

sources.title: Social Network Sites as Networked Publics: Affordances, Dynamics, and Implications
sources.venue: In A Networked Self (Papacharissi, ed.), Routledge: 39-58
sources.note: Canonical taxonomy that names visibility as one of four structural affordances of networked publics, hard-wiring the term into platform-studies vocabulary.

sources.title: Emotion shapes the diffusion of moralized content in social networks
sources.note: Each moral-emotional word increases tweet diffusion ~20%, contagion is largely intra-group — platform-level reward structure for moralized outrage.

sources.title: Visibility: A Category for the Social Sciences
sources.note: Establishes visibility as a relational, double-edged sociological category whose recognition/control outcomes depend on the field of power, scaffolding the thesis's view that visibility is a stake, not neutral exposure.

sources.title: Scale-free networks are rare
sources.note: Empirical analysis of ~1000 networks shows strict scale-freeness is rare especially in social networks, strengthening platform-attribution by undermining the universalist endogeneity defense.

sources.title: If…Then: Algorithmic Power and Politics
sources.venue: Oxford University Press (Oxford Studies in Digital Politics)
sources.note: Book-length argument that algorithmic ranking actively produces forms of acting and knowing, casting platform visibility allocation as a political artifact.

sources.title: YouTube Channels, Uploads and Views: A Statistical Analysis of the Past 10 Years
sources.note: Decade-long YouTube analysis finding 85% of views go to roughly 3% of channels with concentration persisting or worsening over time.

sources.title: Measuring User Influence in Twitter: The Million Follower Fallacy
sources.note: Shows follower count does not equal influence; engagement-converting users differ from raw-popular ones, refining how visibility translates to power.

sources.title: Who Owns the Media? Competition and Concentration in the Mass Media Industry
sources.note: Econometric pushback against McChesney/Bagdikian, arguing legacy media was more competitive than concentration narratives suggest.

sources.title: Exposure to the Russian Internet Research Agency foreign influence campaign on Twitter in the 2016 US election and its relationship to attitudes and voting behavior
sources.note: Russian IRA exposure was extraordinarily concentrated — 1% of users = 70% of exposures, skewed toward strong Republicans, but no detectable attitudinal effect.

sources.title: Blockbusters: Why Big Hits — and Big Risks — Are the Future of the Entertainment Business
sources.note: Empirical refutation of the long-tail thesis using entertainment industry sales data showing the head gets heavier even as the tail lengthens.

sources.title: Rethinking the Public Sphere: A Contribution to the Critique of Actually Existing Democracy
sources.note: Argues the bourgeois public sphere was never inclusive, denying any prior 'equal voice' baseline against which platforms could be measured.

sources.title: Beyond the Hashtags: #Ferguson, #Blacklivesmatter, and the Online Struggle for Offline Justice
sources.venue: Center for Media & Social Impact, American University
sources.note: Large-N study of 40.8M tweets and 40 interviews documenting how activist posts made Ferguson and BLM nationally visible, defending hashtag activism against the slacktivism charge.

sources.title: Can celebrity endorsements affect political outcomes? Evidence from the 2008 US Democratic presidential primary
sources.venue: Journal of Law, Economics, and Organization
sources.note: Pre-platform-era quasi-experiment estimating Oprah's endorsement of Obama yielded approximately 1 million primary votes, establishing causal lineage for influencer effects.

sources.title: The Relevance of Algorithms
sources.venue: In Media Technologies (Gillespie, Boczkowski & Foot, eds.), MIT Press: 167-194
sources.note: Argues public-relevance algorithms constitute 'calculated publics' rather than merely select content, linking mechanism to normative concerns about agenda-setting.

sources.title: Custodians of the Internet: Platforms, Content Moderation, and the Hidden Decisions That Shape Social Media
sources.note: Argues moderation is constitutive of platforms, with hidden, unaccountable editorial decisions shaping public discourse, directly supporting the no-editorial-checks claim.

sources.title: Asymmetric ideological segregation in exposure to political news on Facebook
sources.note: Population-scale analysis of 208M Facebook users showing the algorithm preserves and amplifies asymmetric, conservative-heavy ideological segregation in news exposure.

sources.title: Less than you think: Prevalence and predictors of fake news dissemination on Facebook
sources.note: Only 8.5% shared fake news; concentrated in conservatives and users 65+ (7x more than under-30) — long-tail concentration.

sources.title: The Power of Information Networks: New Directions for Agenda Setting
sources.note: Introduces the Network Agenda Setting (third-level) model showing high-visibility actors shape cognitive bundles of associations, not just topic salience.

sources.title: The Structural Transformation of the Public Sphere: An Inquiry into a Category of Bourgeois Society
sources.note: Locates the public sphere's collapse in commercial mass media long before social media, undermining the 'platforms broke the public sphere' framing.

sources.title: Auditing YouTube's recommendation system for ideologically congenial, extreme, and problematic recommendations
sources.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
sources.note: State-of-the-art 100K-sock-puppet YouTube audit documenting algorithm-attributable ideological congeniality, mild extremity drift, and problematic-channel exposure.

sources.title: Disinformation, 'Fake News' and Influence Campaigns on Twitter
sources.note: Analysis of 10M+ tweets identifying an ultra-dense core of high-visibility accounts that concentrate links to misinformation outlets.

sources.title: Examining the consumption of radical content on YouTube
sources.venue: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences
sources.note: Browser-panel study of >300K Americans concludes user preferences, not YouTube's recommender, drive most radical viewing, countering algorithmic-rabbit-hole claims.

sources.title: Democracy's Fourth Wave? Digital Media and the Arab Spring
sources.note: QCA comparative analysis finds digital media a necessary but not sufficient condition for Arab Spring regime fragility and movement success.

sources.title: The origins and consequences of affective polarization in the United States
sources.note: Affective polarization is real, measurable, and consequential, but multi-causal — partisan media preceded the platform era.

sources.title: #HashtagActivism: Networks of Race and Gender Justice
sources.note: Mixed-methods study of how marginalized communities use Twitter to build counter-narratives and dissent networks, central counterpublic case.

sources.title: A review and provocation: On polarization and platforms
sources.note: Platforms are political institutions; effects mediated by partisan actors exploiting affordances — supports unequal-visibility / elite-capture mediator.

sources.title: What Is Twitter, a Social Network or a News Media?
sources.note: First full Twitter crawl (41.7M users) revealing low reciprocity and non-power-law tail deviations, framing Twitter structurally as news medium not social network.

sources.title: Alternative Influence: Broadcasting the Reactionary Right on YouTube
sources.note: Network analysis of 65 reactionary-right YouTube creators showing influencer collaboration networks function as gatekeeper-free political mobilisation pathways.

sources.title: Using the president's tweets to understand political diversion in the age of social media
sources.note: Quantitative evidence that Trump tweets measurably diverted NYT coverage of the Mueller investigation, undermining accountability via visibility-driven distraction.

sources.title: Public Opinion
sources.note: Foundational defense of elite-mediated democracy showing legacy editorial gatekeeping was deliberately engineered as unequal.

sources.title: The Arab Spring | The Revolutions Were Tweeted: Information Flows During the 2011 Tunisian and Egyptian Revolutions
sources.note: Network analysis of Arab Spring information flows that both supports democratization and documents severe visibility concentration among key activists and journalists.

sources.title: Rethinking Democracy with Social Media
sources.note: Margetts walks back earlier optimism, conceding echo chambers and micro-targeting while still defending tiny-acts participation as democratically renewing.

sources.title: I Tweet Honestly, I Tweet Passionately: Twitter Users, Context Collapse, and the Imagined Audience
sources.note: Argues that performing for an imagined audience under context collapse structurally favors users who master micro-celebrity techniques, contributing to visibility asymmetry.

sources.title: Status Update: Celebrity, Publicity, and Branding in the Social Media Age
sources.note: Ethnography arguing Web 2.0's emancipatory rhetoric concealed a status-and-branding regime that reinforces stratification, directly contesting engagement-equals-democracy claims.

sources.title: The Behaviors and Attitudes of U.S. Adults on Twitter
sources.note: Replication of Wojcik & Hughes finding intensified concentration where top 25% of US adult tweeters produce 97% of tweets.

sources.title: The agenda-setting function of mass media
sources.note: Foundational Chapel Hill study establishing that media transfer issue salience to the public, providing the theoretical basis for visibility-equals-agenda-power.

sources.title: Engagement, user satisfaction, and the amplification of divisive content on social media
sources.note: Pre-registered Twitter audit showing engagement-ranking amplifies out-group hostile content users themselves say they dislike, directly rebutting the engagement-equals-democracy counter.

sources.title: Social influence bias: A randomized experiment
sources.note: Single artificial upvote on a real news platform produces a 25 percent rating increase via asymmetric herding, evidence that platform signals causally and irreversibly bias outcomes upward.

sources.title: Algorithms of Oppression: How Search Engines Reinforce Racism
sources.note: Demonstrates that ad-driven ranking encodes systemic racism and sexism into search visibility, evidencing platform-attributable unequal visibility along race/gender lines.

sources.title: The Filter Bubble: What the Internet Is Hiding From You
sources.note: Popularized 'filter bubble' — personalization algorithms silently narrow what users see, producing tailored information environments hostile to civic argument.

sources.title: Post-Broadcast Democracy: How Media Choice Increases Inequality in Political Involvement and Polarizes Elections
sources.note: Empirically demonstrates cable-era high-choice already produced political knowledge inequality and polarization, predating platforms.

sources.title: Out-group animosity drives engagement on social media
sources.note: Out-group posts shared 2x as often; engagement metrics structurally reward content attacking the out-party with disproportionate visibility.

sources.title: Auditing radicalization pathways on YouTube
sources.note: Audit of 349 YouTube channels and 72M comments finds commenter migration from Alt-Lite to Alt-Right communities via channel-level recommendations.

sources.title: Talk Radio's America: How an Industry Took Over a Political Party That Took Over the United States
sources.note: Documents Limbaugh's ~20M weekly listeners on legacy radio, showing single-voice dominance comparable to contemporary influencers existed pre-platform.

sources.title: Leading the Herd Astray: An Experimental Study of Self-fulfilling Prophecies in an Artificial Cultural Market
sources.note: Follow-up RCT showing inverted popularity rankings became self-fulfilling, demonstrating visibility signals are causal not merely correlational.

sources.title: Auditing algorithms: Research methods for detecting discrimination on internet platforms
sources.venue: 64th Annual Meeting of the International Communication Association, Data and Discrimination Preconference
sources.note: Foundational methodology paper defining five algorithm-audit designs adapted from offline discrimination-audit traditions.

sources.title: Anatomy of News Consumption on Facebook
sources.note: 376M-user Facebook study showing news consumption concentrates on few outlets with more active users being more concentrated, not less.

sources.title: From hashtags to ballots: Conceptualizing political influencers and evaluating their impact on election outcomes
sources.note: Mixed-method study of 2021 German election finding 6% of voters report influencer content informed their vote choice.

sources.title: Social media influencers can increase collective political beliefs and actions: Findings from experiments and a quasi-experimental field study
sources.note: Experimental and quasi-experimental evidence that parasocial bond strength gates influencer effects on political action, identifying intimacy as the active mechanism.

sources.title: Frenemies: How Social Media Polarizes America
sources.note: END framework: Facebook's News Feed blends political signals into ordinary social content, producing identity-based affective polarization via everyday cues.

sources.title: 'Rich-Get-Richer'? Platform Attention and Earnings Inequality using Patreon Earnings Data
sources.note: Cross-platform earnings analysis showing algorithmic platforms intensify Pareto concentration toward capital-income-like inequality with vanishing creator middle class.

sources.title: #Republic: Divided Democracy in the Age of Social Media
sources.note: Same fragmentation/polarization diagnosis applied across web, blogs, and social media since 2001, suggesting the problem is regime-agnostic high choice.

sources.title: The New Visibility
sources.note: Theorizes mediated visibility as a strategically-managed political resource, prefiguring the thesis's claim that visibility is a constitutive feature of modern political power.

sources.title: Social Media, Political Polarization, and Political Disinformation: A Review of the Scientific Literature
sources.note: Tucker-et-al consensus review: case for social media → polarization is methodologically contested; disinformation real but modest quantitative effects.

sources.title: Social Media and the Decision to Participate in Political Protest: Observations From Tahrir Square
sources.note: Survey of 1,200 Tahrir protesters showing social-media use significantly increased first-day protest participation, supporting Arab Spring democratization claims.

sources.title: The political effects of X's feed algorithm
sources.note: Independent seven-week field experiment showing X's algorithmic feed shifts political opinion conservatively with persistent follow-graph changes after treatment.

sources.title: Systematic partisan content skews in TikTok during the 2024 US elections
sources.note: 323-experiment TikTok audit during the 2024 US election shows asymmetric Republican-favoring algorithmic exposure corroborated by user-survey reports.

sources.title: The Platform Society: Public Values in a Connective World
sources.note: Frames platforms as societal infrastructure and asks who anchors public values when private platforms supply democracy's plumbing, directly framing the thesis's dysfunction leg.

sources.title: The agenda-setting power of fake news: A big data analysis of the online media landscape from 2014 to 2016
sources.note: Shows fake news sites act as agenda-setters within partisan online media, evidencing how low-credibility high-visibility sources shape public issues.

sources.title: The Marketplace of Attention: How Audiences Take Shape in a Digital Age
sources.note: Theoretical framework arguing attention markets exhibit 'fragmented concentration' where head-heavy distributions persist alongside cross-outlet overlap.

sources.title: The 'Gate Keeper': A Case Study in the Selection of News
sources.note: Foundational study showing legacy editorial gatekeeping was idiosyncratic and biased rather than principled democratic curation.

sources.title: Big Three (American television) — three-network audience-share consensus statistics
sources.venue: Encyclopedic / industry sources (Wikipedia; EBSCO; Lumen Learning)
sources.note: Quantitative anchor showing NBC/CBS/ABC controlled ~90-95% of U.S. prime-time TV through the 1970s, a stronger concentration than current platform attention regimes.

sources.title: Sizing Up Twitter Users
sources.note: US-representative survey linked to behavior showing the top 10% of adult tweeters produce 80% of all tweets.

sources.title: Who says what to whom on Twitter
sources.note: ~20,000 elite Twitter users account for half of all URL consumption, providing direct empirical anchor for extreme attention concentration on platforms.

sources.title: Auditing political exposure bias: Algorithmic amplification on Twitter/X during the 2024 U.S. presidential election
sources.venue: Proceedings of the 2025 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT)
sources.note: Six-week 2024 X audit documenting that the For You algorithm concentrates exposure on a tiny minority of accounts with strongest skew for right-leaning users.

sources.title: From Content Moderation to Visibility Moderation: A Case Study of Platform Governance on TikTok
sources.note: Names visibility moderation as the dominant lever of platform governance, providing the cleanest statement that visibility allocation is itself a platform act.

sources.title: Communicative intimacies: Influencers and perceived interconnectedness
sources.venue: Ada: A Journal of Gender, New Media, and Technology
sources.note: Introduces perceived interconnectedness typology operationalising how influencers manufacture intimacy-at-scale, the mechanism that converts visibility to influence.

sources.title: Visibility Labour: Engaging with Influencers' Fashion Brands and #OOTD Advertorial Campaigns on Instagram
sources.note: Coins 'visibility labour' to name the quietly exploitative work users perform to make themselves and influencers visible, naming a key mechanism behind attention concentration.

sources.title: Internet Celebrity: Understanding Fame Online
sources.note: Synthesises typologies of internet celebrity and their differential platform fortunes, useful for taxonomy of visibility varieties.

sources.title: Blogs and Bullets: New Media in Contentious Politics
sources.venue: Peaceworks 65, United States Institute of Peace
sources.note: Five-level analytic framework for studying new media in political change, providing methodologically cautious Arab Spring baseline.

sources.title: Blogs and Bullets II: New Media and Conflict after the Arab Spring
sources.venue: Peaceworks 80, United States Institute of Peace
sources.note: Applies five-level framework to four Arab Spring cases finding very different social-media dynamics across contexts, anti-monocausal.

sources.title: Statistical mechanics of complex networks
sources.note: Review showing non-linear preferential attachment (alpha > 1) produces winner-take-all rather than power-law, providing the mathematical hook for super-linear platform amplification.

sources.title: Social media and fake news in the 2016 election
sources.note: Pro-Trump fakes shared ~30M times on Facebook but Fermi-style upper bound makes decisive electoral effect implausible — volume ≠ persuasion.

sources.title: Everyone's an influencer: Quantifying influence on Twitter
sources.note: Tracks 74M Twitter cascades finding follower count predicts influence on average but with enormous variance, complicating the deterministic visibility-equals-power equation.

sources.title: More accounts, fewer links: How algorithmic curation impacts media exposure in Twitter timelines
sources.venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction (CSCW)
sources.note: Sock-puppet audit finds Twitter's algorithm cuts external-link exposure by two-thirds vs. chronological and reshapes content mix relative to the follow graph.

sources.title: Curating quality? How Twitter's timeline algorithm treats different types of news
sources.note: Companion sock-puppet audit finding Twitter's algorithm does not uniformly favor high-quality news; topic-level amplification effects vary.

sources.title: Emergence of Scaling in Random Networks
sources.note: Introduces the preferential-attachment model as the mathematical foundation for why growth-plus-rich-get-richer linking yields scale-free follower distributions.

sources.title: Tweeting from left to right: Is online political communication more than an echo chamber?
sources.note: Echo chambers are issue-dependent: political topics produce echo-chamber dynamics but non-political topics produce cross-cutting national conversation.

sources.title: Social media, echo chambers, and political polarization
sources.note: Echo chambers real but smaller and less universal than claimed; recalibrate downward on bubbles, upward on incentive-driven amplification.

sources.title: A New Era of Minimal Effects? The Changing Foundations of Political Communication
sources.note: Argues shared news experience under broadcast was an artifact of low choice; selective exposure and polarization emerged with media abundance pre-platform.

sources.title: The perils of audience capture
sources.note: Non-academic essay coining audience capture as a feedback loop where audiences replace editors, central to the no-editorial-checks premise.

sources.title: Anxiety, Panic and Self-Optimization: Inequalities and the YouTube Algorithm
sources.note: Documents affective costs of constant self-optimization under opaque algorithms, deepening the normative case against algorithmic visibility regimes.

sources.title: Managing Visibility on YouTube through Algorithmic Gossip
sources.note: Coins 'algorithmic gossip' and shows YouTube produces a discriminatory visibility hierarchy aligned with advertiser interests, with gendered consequences.

sources.title: An empirical investigation of personalization factors on TikTok
sources.venue: Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2022 (WWW)
sources.note: TikTok For You sock-puppet audit ranks follow > like > view-rate as personalization signals and documents filter-bubble dynamics.

sources.title: Crowdsourced audit of Twitter's recommender systems
sources.note: Crowdsourced X For You audit characterizing the in-network vs. out-of-network split and bias toward high-engagement, emotionally charged, low-credibility content.

sources.title: Greater Internet use is not associated with faster growth in political polarization among US demographic groups
sources.note: Polarization rose most among demographics least likely to use social media (75+) — casts doubt on primary causal role for digital media.

sources.title: The Use of Twitter Hashtags in the Formation of Ad Hoc Publics
sources.venue: 6th European Consortium for Political Research General Conference
sources.note: Introduces ad hoc publics around hashtags; own data show heavy skew in hashtag participation, double-edged for democratization claims.

sources.title: Are filter bubbles real?
sources.note: Sustained critique arguing filter-bubble and echo-chamber metaphors are empirically thin, framing polarization as human- rather than platform-driven.

sources.title: Goodbye Pareto Principle, Hello Long Tail: The Effect of Search Costs on the Concentration of Product Sales
sources.note: Same-retailer online versus catalog comparison showing internet channels can reduce sales concentration via lower search costs, steelmanning the de-concentration counter.

sources.title: The Algorithmic Imaginary: Exploring the Ordinary Affects of Facebook Algorithms
sources.note: Introduces the algorithmic imaginary, showing that users' folk theories feed back into platform behavior and thus form part of the visibility-allocation mechanism.

sources.title: Zipf's Law Across Social Media
sources.note: Tests power-law fits across eight major platforms confirming Zipf-distributed follower concentration with platform-level variation in steepness.

sources.title: Tiered Governance and Demonetization: The Shifting Terms of Labor and Compensation in the Platform Economy
sources.note: Shows YouTube administers visibility through tiered, bureaucratically-stratified rules that protect already-visible creators while denying smaller ones recourse.

sources.title: Networks of Outrage and Hope: Social Movements in the Internet Age
sources.note: Comparative theory of 2011 networked movements as new collective action enabled by autonomous communication networks escaping gatekeepers.

sources.title: Complex contagions and the weakness of long ties
sources.note: Complex contagions requiring reinforced exposure spread through clustered networks not long ties, suggesting hub-broadcast may not drive politically-consequential behavior.

sources.title: The spread of behavior in an online social network experiment
sources.note: Experimental confirmation that health-behavior adoption spreads faster in clustered than random topologies, complicating broadcast-hub models of political mobilization.

sources.title: Analyzing the Video Popularity Characteristics of Large-Scale User-Generated Content Systems
sources.note: YouTube/Daum measurement confirming a Pareto distribution where 10% of videos capture 80% of views.

sources.title: Power-law distributions in empirical data
sources.note: Methodological paper exposing widespread false positives in power-law claims, requiring re-evaluation of older social-media scale-free assertions.

sources.title: The rise of Twitter in the political campaign: Searching for intermedia agenda-setting effects in the presidential primary
sources.note: Time-series evidence that candidates with strong social media visibility pull mainstream coverage in their direction on selected campaign topics.

sources.title: Why Voice Matters: Culture and Politics After Neoliberalism
sources.note: Normative argument that voice is the central democratic value and neoliberal media systems systematically deny it, a yardstick for evaluating platforms.

sources.title: Media and Power
sources.note: Critical historiography showing 'broadcast era as democratic' is a Whiggish liberal narrative that erases ownership concentration.

sources.title: From editors to algorithms: A values-based approach to understanding story selection in the Facebook News Feed
sources.note: Document analysis of Facebook patents and filings identifying nine ranking values, reframing News Feed curation as editorial without journalistic norms.

sources.title: The Public and Its Problems
sources.note: Counter to Lippmann; the participatory Deweyan ideal was never realized under legacy media, supporting platform proponents' 'more voices = more democracy' line.

sources.title: Cumulative advantage as a mechanism for inequality: A review of theoretical and empirical developments
sources.note: Distinguishes weak (path-dependent) from strong (exponential) cumulative advantage, mapping directly onto whether platforms implement linear or super-linear amplification.

sources.title: (Not) Getting Paid to Do What You Love: Gender and Aspirational Labor in the Social Media Economy
sources.note: Introduces 'aspirational labor' to show the promise of visibility is itself a labor-extraction mechanism that disproportionately exploits women creators.

sources.title: The Nested Precarities of Creative Labor on Social Media
sources.note: Identifies 'algorithmic precarity' as a distinctive form of insecurity nested within capitalism, markets, and platform features.

sources.title: Platform Governance at the Margins: Social Media Creators' Experiences with Algorithmic (In)visibility
sources.note: 30 interviews show marginalized creators experience algorithmic governance as patterned suppression, countering utopian 'anyone can be visible' framings.

sources.title: Digitally Enabled Social Change: Activism in the Internet Age
sources.note: Empirical study of online petitions and boycotts identifying reduced costs and decreased need for co-presence as key democratizing affordances.

sources.title: Should You Invest in the Long Tail?
sources.note: Pre-book Rhapsody and Nielsen analysis showing the top 1% of titles capture 32% of plays, anchoring empirical opposition to long-tail claims.

sources.title: I always assumed that I wasn't really that close to [her]: Reasoning about Invisible Algorithms in News Feeds
sources.note: Empirically shows most users are unaware News Feed is curated; once exposed they actively reshape algorithmic visibility, complicating black-box accounts with user agency.

sources.title: Discipline and Punish: The Birth of the Prison
sources.note: Provides the panoptic foil that platform-studies scholars (notably Bucher) invert: on platforms the disciplinary threat is invisibility rather than constant visibility.

sources.title: Presidential endorsements as voter cues: Evidence from the 2022 midterm elections
sources.note: Survey-experiment showing Trump endorsements had a negative net effect on support, demonstrating high-visibility cues are powerful but signed by audience reaction.

sources.title: The effects of algorithmic content selection on user engagement with news on Twitter
sources.note: Quasi-experiment of Twitter's 2016 default switch finds rich-get-richer engagement gains concentrated in already-popular outlets.

sources.title: The Politics of 'Platforms'
sources.note: Genealogy of the term 'platform' showing its strategic ambiguity allows providers to claim openness while evading editorial accountability.

sources.title: Small Change: Why the Revolution Will Not Be Tweeted
sources.note: Popular skeptical essay arguing social media supports only weak ties while high-risk activism requires strong ties, framing the public debate.

sources.title: Exposure to untrustworthy websites in the 2016 US election
sources.note: Untrustworthy-site visits are small share of consumption, concentrated in pro-attitudinal subset, often referred from Facebook — concentration not diffusion.

sources.title: The power of message networks: A big-data analysis of the Network Agenda Setting Model and issue ownership
sources.note: Empirical NAS test in 2012 US election showing traditional news media still anchor the networked agenda on Twitter, complicating the no-editorial-checks claim.

sources.title: Facebook tried to make its platform a healthier place. It got angrier instead
sources.note: Facebook Files reporting on the 2018 MSI change provides primary-source platform evidence that engagement-ranking shifted publishers toward divisive content.

sources.title: The 'Uncensored War': The Media and Vietnam
sources.note: Documents Hallin's spheres model showing legacy media systematically narrowed visible political opinion to elite consensus.

sources.title: The political influence of lifestyle influencers? Examining the relationship between aspirational social media use and anti-expert attitudes and beliefs
sources.note: Demonstrates parasocial bonds to lifestyle influencers spill over into anti-expert political beliefs, countering the content-over-creators rebuttal.

sources.title: Influence you can trust? Exploring trust in social media influencers for political information
sources.note: Survey evidence that trust in political influencers is unequally distributed and correlates with lower election-related belief accuracy.

sources.title: Opening Closed Regimes: What Was the Role of Social Media During the Arab Spring?
sources.venue: Project on Information Technology and Political Islam Working Paper 2011.1, University of Washington
sources.note: Analyzes 3M+ tweets showing online revolutionary conversation spiked before offline Arab Spring events and spread democratic ideas across borders.

sources.title: The Influencer Industry: The Quest for Authenticity on Social Media
sources.note: Industry history showing visibility is not just algorithmically allocated but laundered through industrial constructs like authenticity and brand fit.

sources.title: Measuring misinformation in video search platforms: An audit study on YouTube
sources.venue: Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction (CSCW)
sources.note: Sock-puppet audit finds YouTube watch-history personalization amplifies misinformation while pure demographic personalization does not.

sources.title: The Marketplace of Ideas: A Legitimizing Myth
sources.note: Legal-theoretical critique establishing that the marketplace of ideas was never empirically equal, scaffolding structural critiques of platform deregulation.

sources.title: Red Media, Blue Media: Evidence of Ideological Selectivity in Media Use
sources.note: Experimental evidence that ideological self-sorting in cable news preceded social media, suggesting platforms inherited rather than invented echo chambers.

sources.title: Silenced on social media: The gatekeeping functions of shadowbans in the American Twitterverse
sources.note: Large-N empirical audit of four Twitter shadowban types showing opaque, temporally unstable suppression as the flip side of algorithmic amplification.

sources.title: The MoveOn Effect: The Unexpected Transformation of American Political Advocacy
sources.note: Counterweight to influencer-individualism showing digital political power also flows through low-overhead networked organisations.

sources.title: Populist everyday politics in the (mediatized) age of social media: The case of Instagram celebrity advocacy
sources.note: Multimodal analysis showing platforms structurally encourage populist-style celebrity advocacy through performances of ordinariness.

sources.title: Quantifying social influence in an online cultural market
sources.note: Replicates MusicLab and shows social-influence effects grow as markets mature, implying cumulative-advantage intensifies over a platform's lifecycle.

sources.title: The science of fake news
sources.note: 16-author Science Policy Forum defining fake news as platform-relevant democratic concern requiring multi-front response.

sources.title: Setting the record straighter on shadow banning
sources.note: Graph-topological audit of >2.5M Twitter profiles statistically rejecting the platform's bug-explanation for shadowbans and showing epidemic-like propagation.

sources.title: Algorithmic extremism: Examining YouTube's rabbit hole of radicalization
sources.note: Non-personalized YouTube audit finds the recommender directs traffic toward mainstream and away from fringe content, contesting the rabbit-hole thesis.

sources.title: Radical bubbles on YouTube? Revisiting algorithmic extremism with personalised recommendations
sources.note: Personalized persona-bot audit finds only moderate YouTube filter-bubble effects and weak extremism amplification, reinforcing the anti-rabbit-hole view.

sources.title: Social media, news consumption, and polarization: Evidence from a field experiment
sources.note: Random Facebook news subscriptions: counter-attitudinal exposure reduced negative affect (contra Bail 2018) — backfire is not universal.

sources.title: Parasocial interactions and relationships with media characters — An inventory of 60 years of research
sources.note: Systematic review of 250+ PSI/PSR studies establishing the empirical robustness of parasocial bonds as drivers of attitude change.

sources.title: Hatespeech or Tatespeech? Andrew Tate and the rise of the radical misogynist
sources.note: Discourse analysis showing manfluencer content acts as a radicalisation pathway producing measurable off-platform attitude effects.

sources.title: Coordinating a multi-platform disinformation campaign: Internet Research Agency activity on three U.S. social media platforms, 2015 to 2017
sources.note: Quantitative evidence that small numbers of coordinated accounts can engineer visibility advantages across platforms and produce outsized agenda effects.

sources.title: Networks of Audience Overlap in the Consumption of Digital News
sources.note: comScore panel-based audience-overlap networks showing a head-heavy legacy core surrounded by diverse peripheral sources, bridging Hindman and Webster perspectives.

sources.title: To see and be seen: Celebrity practice on Twitter
sources.note: Analyses celebrity as a practice on Twitter showing how the appearance of intimacy concentrates visibility and influence in few users.

sources.title: Networked Privacy: How Teenagers Negotiate Context in Social Media
sources.note: Shows visibility/privacy are negotiated at the network level rather than the individual level, complicating simple 'everything is visible' framings.

sources.title: Communication Revolution: Critical Junctures and the Future of Media
sources.note: Frames media regimes (broadcast, internet) as contingent policy artifacts rather than natural states, useful for periodizing visibility regimes.

sources.title: The Political Economy of Media: Enduring Issues, Emerging Dilemmas
sources.note: Synthesizes two decades of work showing legacy professional journalism was a legitimizing strategy for corporate ownership, not a democratic ideal.

sources.title: Setting the Agenda: The Mass Media and Public Opinion (2nd ed.)
sources.note: Synthesis of 40 years of agenda-setting research arguing the function persists across digital fragmentation but is now distributed across multiple actors.

sources.title: The Matthew effect in science
sources.note: Coined Matthew effect showing cumulative advantage produces normative misalignment between visibility and merit, refuting the visibility-equals-democratic-choice rebuttal.

sources.title: The Net Delusion: The Dark Side of Internet Freedom
sources.note: Polemic against cyber-utopianism arguing authoritarian regimes use the same tools as activists, undercutting naive Twitter-revolution democratization claims.

sources.title: YouTube Regrets: A crowdsourced investigation into YouTube's recommendation algorithm
sources.note: Largest crowdsourced YouTube audit (37K users, 190 countries) shows 71% of regretted videos were recommended and that policy-violating content amassed 160M+ views before removal.

sources.title: Echo chambers and epistemic bubbles
sources.note: Distinguishes epistemic bubbles from echo chambers, identifying trust manipulation by high-visibility creators as the engine of resistant epistemic dysfunction.

sources.title: Participation Inequality: The 90-9-1 Rule for Social Features
sources.note: Popularized the canonical 90-9-1 heuristic describing Zipf-distributed participation curves on social platforms.

sources.title: A Meritocratic Network Formation Model for the Rise of Social Media Influencers
sources.note: Network model showing even meritocratic search for top creators yields Zipf-distributed follower counts, partially steelmanning structural inevitability of concentration.

sources.title: Affective Publics: Sentiment, Technology, and Politics
sources.note: Theorizes affective publics, arguing the feeling and rhythm of Twitter news constitutes political participation under-recognized by deliberative theory.

sources.title: The Black Box Society: The Secret Algorithms That Control Money and Information
sources.note: Shows algorithmic opacity removes editorial accountability across search, finance, and reputation, supporting the thesis's 'no editorial checks' leg.

sources.title: Social Media and Democracy: The State of the Field, Prospects for Reform
sources.note: Multi-author edited volume mapping empirical and regulatory state of disinformation, hate speech, advertising, foreign influence and reform proposals.

sources.title: Gaming the System: Platform Paternalism and the Politics of Algorithmic Visibility
sources.note: Documents platforms' paternalistic discourse that selectively denounces engagement tactics they quietly encourage, undermining the engagement-equals-democracy counter.

sources.title: Newspapers Fact Sheet (and Loss of Newspapers and Readers — UNC/Hussman)
sources.venue: Pew Research Center; UNC Hussman School
sources.note: Quantitative baseline showing severe pre-platform newspaper concentration with most U.S. cities reduced to single-newspaper towns by the late 20th century.

sources.title: A general theory of bibliometric and other cumulative advantage processes
sources.venue: Journal of the American Society for Information Science
sources.note: Earliest formal treatment of cumulative advantage, establishing that power-law concentration predates social media and should be treated as the null hypothesis.

sources.title: Bowling Alone: The Collapse and Revival of American Community
sources.note: Attributes pre-platform civic erosion partly to broadcast TV, disrupting any 'healthy civic baseline that platforms broke' assumption.

sources.title: The Economics of Superstars
sources.note: Foundational economic theory showing imperfect substitution plus joint consumption translates small talent gaps into enormous reward inequality as reach scales.

sources.title: The Shadow Banning Controversy: Perceived Governance and Algorithmic Folklore
sources.note: Coins 'algorithmic folklore' and demonstrates that even perceived invisibility disciplines user behavior, extending mechanism arguments beyond verifiable enforcement.

sources.title: Culture, Inc.: The Corporate Takeover of Public Expression
sources.note: Argues legacy editorial gatekeeping was itself corporate consciousness-management, complicating the claim that platforms lost a neutral editorial check.

sources.title: Discovering the News: A Social History of American Newspapers
sources.note: Shows journalistic objectivity was a contingent ~70-year regime, not the natural state media should return to.

sources.title: Camgirls: Celebrity and Community in the Age of Social Networks
sources.note: Coins 'micro-celebrity' and supplies the genealogy for strategic-self-presentation scholarship that the influencer-visibility literature builds on.

sources.title: The Political Power of Social Media: Technology, the Public Sphere, and Political Change
sources.note: Influential essay arguing social media's democratic value is long-run civil-society formation and the dictator's dilemma, not protest spectacle.

sources.title: Gatekeeping Theory
sources.note: Synthesizes 60 years of gatekeeping research showing legacy editorial 'checks' were institutional interests, not neutral filters.

sources.title: Rethinking the Black Public Sphere: An Alternative Vocabulary for Multiple Public Spheres
sources.note: Pre-platform typology of enclave, counterpublic, and satellite publics that scaffolds later hashtag-activism scholarship.

sources.title: The Creation of the Media: Political Origins of Modern Communications
sources.note: Argues U.S. media regimes including broadcast oligopoly were active policy decisions, supporting the view that visibility structures are constructed.

sources.title: Strategic Invisibility: How Creators Manage the Risks and Constraints of Online Hyper(In)Visibility
sources.note: Coins 'hyper(in)visibility' and complicates visibility-good/invisibility-bad binary by showing strategic invisibility as resistance for marginalized creators.

sources.title: Celebrity politicians: Popular culture and political representation
sources.venue: British Journal of Politics and International Relations
sources.note: Theoretical typology arguing celebrity politics is not categorically illegitimate, providing nuance against blanket-dysfunctional claims.

sources.title: Intermedia attribute agenda setting between the U.S. mainstream newspapers and Twitter: A two-study analysis of the paradigm and driving forces of the agenda flow
sources.note: Elite, high-visibility Twitter users disproportionately drive mainstream newspaper agendas, validating premise 3 at the attribute level.

sources.title: Big Questions for Social Media Big Data: Representativeness, Validity and Other Methodological Pitfalls
sources.venue: Proceedings of the International AAAI Conference on Web and Social Media
sources.note: Methodological caution that Twitter samples are non-random and unrepresentative, applying to both pro- and anti-democratization claims.

sources.title: Telecommunications Act of 1996 — Radio Consolidation Data (FCC Tristani statement; Federal Communications Law Journal)
sources.venue: FCC / Federal Communications Law Journal / Wikipedia
sources.note: Regulatory baseline showing legacy radio concentration was severe and worsening trajectory before social media inherited the environment.

sources.title: The Culture of Connectivity: A Critical History of Social Media
sources.note: Shows platforms engineer commercially-tractable 'connectivity' through coded interfaces and popularity metrics under the guise of facilitating sociality.

sources.title: Me, myself, and the influencer: Examining how parasocial interaction and self-congruence with social media influencers affects news media trust
sources.note: Survey study quantifying substitution between influencer-based trust and legacy news trust depending on identity congruence.

sources.title: Influentials, networks, and public opinion formation
sources.note: Simulation finds cascades driven by easily-influenced critical-mass adopters more than by influencers, partially rebutting the visibility-to-political-power pipeline.

sources.title: Novelty and Collective Attention
sources.note: Models Digg attention dynamics with stretched-exponential decay, showing discounted multiplicative processes produce heavy-tailed popularity distributions.

sources.title: Power-law distribution of the World Wide Web
sources.note: Early empirical critique of pure BA model showing newer WWW nodes can have high connectivity, bringing intrinsic content quality back as partial explanation of visibility.

sources.title: Imagined Communities: Reflections on the Origin and Spread of Nationalism
sources.note: Shows the 'shared public' invoked nostalgically was itself a constructed product of print capitalism, never a natural political fact.

sources.title: Authentic: The Politics of Ambivalence in a Brand Culture
sources.note: Provides the cultural-political backdrop of brand culture in which self-branded visibility regimes become the dominant online mode.

sources.title: Political Rumors: Why We Accept Misinformation and How to Fight It
sources.note: Synthesis on political misinformation acceptance and correction — corrections work on beliefs but often not on behavior.

sources.title: Competition and multiscaling in evolving networks
sources.note: Extends preferential attachment with node fitness, showing late high-quality entrants can overtake entrenched hubs and complicating pure path-dependence stories.

sources.title: A theory of fads, fashion, custom, and cultural change as informational cascades
sources.note: Decision-theoretic model showing rational Bayesian agents cascade on observed predecessors, providing a rationality-compatible foundation for visibility-driven herding.

sources.title: Social Network Sites: Definition, History, and Scholarship
sources.note: Defining lexical reference for the SNS category, foundational for any later platform-studies argument.

sources.title: The Longer Tail: The Changing Shape of Amazon's Sales Distribution Curve
sources.note: Update on Amazon's long tail showing niche books reach 36.7% of sales while power-law slope steepens for the obscure tail.

sources.title: Research Commentary — Long Tails vs. Superstars: The Effect of Information Technology on Product Variety and Sales Concentration Patterns
sources.note: Reconciliation paper arguing long tail and superstar patterns coexist as a barbell with heavier head, longer tail, and thinner middle.

sources.title: Habermas and the Public Sphere
sources.note: Standard edited collection on post-Habermasian consensus that the original public sphere was partial and historically contingent.

sources.title: The Internet, Public Spheres, and Political Communication: Dispersion and Deliberation
sources.note: Pre-Facebook argument that the internet extends and pluralizes the public sphere, precursor to affective-publics scholarship.

sources.title: Power laws and rich-get-richer phenomena (Chapter 18)
sources.venue: Networks, Crowds, and Markets, Cambridge University Press
sources.note: Textbook synthesis integrating preferential attachment, information cascades, and platform concentration into an accessible combined framework.

sources.title: The Printing Press as an Agent of Change: Communications and Cultural Transformations in Early-Modern Europe
sources.note: Provides long-baseline analogy that communication revolutions historically democratize access and produce political dysfunction simultaneously.

sources.title: Threshold models of collective behavior
sources.note: Foundational threshold-model showing cascade outcomes are highly sensitive to small initial conditions, providing micro-foundation for Salganik-style unpredictability.

sources.title: Big social data analytics in journalism and mass communication: Comparing dictionary-based text analysis and unsupervised topic modeling
sources.note: Methodological infrastructure paper demonstrating computational tools that enable empirical measurement of cross-platform agenda-setting at scale.

sources.title: Horton and Wohl revisited: Exploring viewers' experience of parasocial interaction
sources.note: Modern operationalisation of PSI showing bodily-address cues drive interaction-like cognitive engagement, applicable to selfie-cam politics.

sources.title: Social Media and Everyday Politics
sources.note: Defends memes, selfies, and casual hashtag use as legitimate political participation against narrow formal-politics framings.

sources.title: Testing the Marketplace of Ideas
sources.note: Empirical test finding the marketplace's truth-tracking function is at best partial, reinforcing that both legacy and platform regimes fail.

sources.title: Rare and everywhere: Perspectives on scale-free networks
sources.note: Defends heavy-tailed distributions as robust regularity even where strict power-laws fail, softening but not dissolving the cumulative-advantage account.

sources.title: Analytic Activism: Digital Listening and the New Political Strategy
sources.note: Argues analytics-driven advocacy functions as a new editorial logic optimised for engagement rather than deliberation.

sources.title: Role of the New Media in the Arab Spring
sources.note: Short reflection that new media played a critical role in horizontal mobilization where open media and civil society were absent.

sources.title: Celebrity politics: The politics of the late modernity?
sources.note: Review distinguishing platform-attributable effects from broader late-modern personalisation trends, useful for premise 2 calibration.

sources.title: The Radio Right: How a Band of Broadcasters Took on the Federal Government and Built the Modern Conservative Movement
sources.note: Flagged as unverified in the cluster; referenced as context for talk-radio concentration but Rosenwald is the primary verified citation.

sources.title: Digital Prefigurative Participation: The Entwinement of Online Communication and Offline Participation in Protest Events
sources.note: Three-year study of environmental protests showing online communication prefigures but does not cause offline participation.

sources.title: A brief history of generative models for power law and lognormal distributions
sources.note: Surveys overlap between power-law and lognormal generative models, cautioning that observable distributions cannot distinguish preferential attachment from multiplicative growth.

sources.title: Power laws, Pareto distributions and Zipf's law
sources.note: Pedagogical review showing many mechanisms can generate the same heavy-tailed distribution, warning against inferring causation from shape alone.

sources.title: Why the backfire effect does not explain the durability of political misperceptions
sources.note: Re-casts the backfire effect literature — durability of misperceptions has other mechanisms beyond correction-backfire.

sources.title: You really are a great big sister – Parasocial relationships, credibility, and the moderating role of audience comments in influencer marketing
sources.note: Demonstrates parasocial relationships function as credibility multipliers, the mechanism by which visibility translates to influence.

sources.title: Politainment
sources.venue: The International Encyclopedia of Media Effects (Wiley-Blackwell)
sources.note: Encyclopaedia entry consolidating politainment research and noting its normative concerns for deliberative engagement in the platform era.

sources.title: Microcelebrity and the Branded Self
sources.venue: In A Companion to New Media Dynamics (Hartley, Burgess, Bruns, eds.), Wiley-Blackwell: 346-354
sources.note: Restates micro-celebrity as an industrial branded-self logic, setting up Marwick's Status Update.

sources.title: On a class of skew distribution functions
sources.note: Foundational Yule-Simon process showing many heavy-tailed distributions (wealth, word frequency, city sizes) share a single attachment mechanism, generalizing the baseline skew.

sources.title: Twitter as a way for celebrities to communicate with fans: Implications for the study of parasocial interaction
sources.note: Empirical mapping of Twitter PSI cues that strengthen fan attachment, useful for understanding politicians' Twitter strategies.

sources.title: Rabble-Rousers in the New King's Court: Algorithmic effects on account visibility in pre-X Twitter
sources.note: Replicates pro-right algorithmic amplification on Twitter and argues account-behavioral correlates (not ideology per se) drive much of the effect.

sources.title: Scholarly uptake of audience capture (placeholder)
sources.note: Placeholder entry flagged UNVERIFIED noting limited peer-reviewed academic treatment of audience capture as a key term.

sources.title: Celebrity Politics: Image and Identity in Contemporary Political Communications
sources.note: Book-length treatment showing celebrity capital is a structural feature of modern democratic campaigning, prefiguring the influencer turn.

sources.title: The elusive backfire effect: Mass attitudes' steadfast factual adherence
sources.note: Backfire effect is not robust empirically — corrections generally update beliefs but updates fade and don't change behavior.
